summary: This is a curated links post where O'Neil shares articles on big data hype, economic inequality, Ferguson reporting, and class mobility. She highlights Michael Jordan's skepticism about big data (noting her own similar prediction), critiques Janet Yellen's inequality speech for avoiding the Fed's role in QE, and points to research showing poor kids who 'do everything right' still fare worse than rich kids who don't. The post functions as a window into O'Neil's intellectual network and concerns circa 2014.
key_points: Machine learning experts like Michael Jordan were warning about the 'big data bubble' - O'Neil claims she saw this coming too
Janet Yellen acknowledged inequality but conveniently avoided discussing the Fed's own role through quantitative easing
The Fed has been 'overreaching with QE' but can't be expected to do Congress's job on inequality
Class mobility data shows structural barriers trump individual effort - poor kids doing everything right still lose to rich kids doing everything wrong
money_quotes: One thing she didn't mention: quantitative easing, or anything else the Fed has actual control over.
Plus she hid behind the language of economics in terms of how much to care about any of this or what she or anyone else could do.
The Fed has, in my opinion, already been overreaching with QE and we can't expect it to do the job of Congress.
Poor kids who do everything right don't do better than rich kids who do everything wrong.
key_insight: Powerful institutions like the Fed acknowledge systemic problems like inequality while carefully avoiding discussion of their own complicity in creating them.
